aliases: Young Cassava Leaves in Coconut Milk

english: Young Cassava Leaves in Coconut Milk

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Pucuk Ubi Masak Lemak. Mapped to the closest parent class 'cooked vegetable dish' (FOODON_03315702) as the most defensible available anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for tapioca/cassava leaf coconut milk dish. A00LC (cooked leaf vegetables) is the closest parent category available in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this traditional Malay vegetable preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Pucuk Ubi Masak Lemak is approximately 150 g, typically ladled as a side dish alongside steamed white rice.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritional Composition of Selected Malaysian Dishes — IMR Malaysia MyFCD / FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Tables for Southeast Asia
nutrient_source.organization: Institute for Medical Research (IMR) Malaysia — MyFCD; FAO/INFOODS
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single MyFCD entry exists for Pucuk Ubi Masak Lemak as a composite dish. Values are adapted from IMR MyFCD entries for cooked cassava/tapioca leaves and coconut milk-based vegetable dishes, cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA composition data for Southeast Asian leafy vegetable preparations. Saturated fat estimated from coconut milk contribution (~60% of total fat as saturated fat, consistent with FAO/INFOODS SEA coconut milk data). Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat estimated from component proportions; marked -1 where direct composite data unavailable. Vitamin B12 set to -1 as its presence is ingredient-dependent (only present if dried anchovies or dried prawns are used) and no confirmed composite value is available from approved sources. Cholesterol and water data not available in composite form from approved sources; set to -1.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values — Atkinson et al. 2008; University of Sydney GI Database — analogous leafy vegetable dishes in coconut milk
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Pucuk Ubi Masak Lemak in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 25 (low) based on analogous cooked leafy vegetable dishes with coconut milk from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Tapioca leaves are very low in available carbohydrate; coconut milk fat further attenuates glycemic response. Glycemic load per 150 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (25 × 10.8) / 100 = 2.7.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (180 mg/100 g) and high saturated fat from coconut milk; sodium increases further if dried anchovies or dried prawns are added
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100 g or less per meal, request preparation without dried anchovies or dried prawns to reduce sodium, and consume this dish no more than 2–3 times per week as part of a DASH-aligned dietary pattern.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~25) and moderate dietary fibre (2.5 g/100 g) support glycemic control; fat from coconut milk further attenuates postprandial glucose rise
type2_diabetes.guidance: Pucuk Ubi Masak Lemak is a suitable vegetable side dish for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes at a standard 150 g serving, provided total meal carbohydrate from accompanying rice is monitored and kept within the individual's carbohydrate budget.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from coconut milk (~5.1 g/100 g) contributes to LDL-raising saturated fat intake; however, the dish is rich in potassium (320 mg/100 g), vitamin K, and antioxidant micronutrients from tapioca leaves
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish to small portions (100 g) and moderate frequency (once or twice per week), preferring preparations with reduced coconut milk to lower saturated fat intake while retaining the micronutrient benefits of the tapioca leaves.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Turmeric (curcumin) has documented anti-inflammatory properties; tapioca leaves provide antioxidant vitamins A and C; however, high saturated fat may be mildly pro-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: This dish may be consumed regularly by individuals with rheumatoid arthritis for its anti-inflammatory turmeric content and antioxidant micronutrients, but portion size should be moderated to limit saturated fat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pucuk Ubi Masak Lemak is a traditional Malay and Indonesian vegetable dish made from tender young tapioca (cassava) leaves simmered in a rich, golden coconut milk gravy. The leaves are first blanched to remove bitterness and reduce cyanogenic glycoside content, then slow-cooked in coconut milk seasoned with a freshly pounded spice paste of turmeric, shallots, garlic, and bird's eye chilies. Optional additions of dried anchovies (ikan bilis) or dried prawns introduce a savory umami depth. The dish has a creamy, lightly spiced flavor with earthy undertones from the greens and a warm golden color from fresh turmeric. It is a quintessential kampung (village-style) dish across Malaysia and Indonesia, typically served as a side dish alongside steamed white rice and salted fish. Nutritionally, it provides moderate protein (3.8 g/100 g), significant fat from coconut milk (8.5 g/100 g, predominantly saturated at ~5.1 g/100 g), and a low carbohydrate load (7.2 g/100 g). It is notably rich in vitamin A (450 mcg RAE/100 g), vitamin K (120 mcg/100 g), potassium (320 mg/100 g), calcium (95 mg/100 g), and iron (2.2 mg/100 g), making it a micronutrient-dense vegetable accompaniment. Its low glycemic index (~25) makes it suitable for individuals managing blood glucose. The anti-inflammatory properties of turmeric add functional food value. Clinically, sodium content (180 mg/100 g) warrants attention for hypertension patients, and saturated fat content is relevant for cardiovascular risk management.

confidence_reason: Nutritional values are adapted from IMR Malaysia MyFCD and FAO/INFOODS SEA data for cooked cassava/tapioca leaf dishes and coconut milk-based vegetable preparations, as no single approved-source entry exists for this exact composite dish. Saturated fat is estimated from coconut milk component proportion. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for similar leafy vegetable coconut milk dishes. Medium confidence reflects the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, cholesterol, and moisture data for this composite traditional preparation are not available as a single entry in IMR MyFCD or FAO/INFOODS SEA as of 2026. Vitamin B12 is set to -1 because its presence is ingredient-dependent (only present if dried anchovies or dried prawns are used) and no confirmed composite value is available from approved sources. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0: tapioca leaves and coconut milk contain negligible vitamin D; confirmed negligible per FAO/INFOODS SEA data for plant-based coconut milk dishes. 3. Saturated fat (5.1 g/100 g) is estimated as approximately 60% of total fat, consistent with FAO/INFOODS SEA coconut milk fatty acid profile data. 4. The dietary_tag 'low_sodium' in the input is considered inaccurate given 180 mg sodium/100 g and the potential for higher sodium with dried anchovies; this tag has been removed from the output classification. 5. Glycemic load per serving calculated as GL = (25 × 10.8 g available carbohydrate per 150 g serving) / 100 = 2.7. 6. FoodOn FOODON_03315702 and FoodEx2 A00LC are closest-parent matches; no exact ontology code exists for this specific dish in any approved ontology as of 2026. Gap notes provided for all closest-parent matches.

Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match_type values — validator rejected 'broader' as not in allowed set. Changed to 'closest_parent' for both foodon_match_type and foodex2_match_type.

Iteration 3: Confirmed foodon_match_type and foodex2_match_type are set to 'closest_parent' (valid allowed value). Updated gap notes to reference 'closest parent' terminology consistently. All other sections preserved from iteration 2.
